aliases: Deep-fried smashed chicken with steamed rice

english: Deep-fried smashed chicken with steamed rice

region_of_origin: Indonesia (widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for ayam penyet; 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03311069) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for deep-fried smashed chicken with rice is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for ayam penyet with rice. A00LC (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary ingredient anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for ayam penyet or Indonesian smashed chicken dish as of 2024.

main_ingredients: cooking oil (palm oil or coconut oil)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of ayam penyet with rice in Singapore typically weighs approximately 500 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried smashed chicken, steamed white rice, sambal, and accompaniments such as tempeh, tofu, cucumber, and cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Penyet with Rice — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 193.0 kcal, protein 11.28 g, fat 8.7 g, carbohydrate 17.39 g, sugar 2.14 g, sodium 242.0 mg, dietary fibre 1.16 g) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id F05100003, confidence 0.95). This is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Secondary micronutrients (calcium 14.55 mg, iron 0.73 mg, cholesterol 45.45 mg, saturated fat 3.64 g, trans fat 0.09 g, vitamin C 2.73 mg) were present in the input record at confidence 0.70 from a non-approved secondary source; these values are retained as indicative estimates pending verification against an approved primary source and are flagged accordingly in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ayam penyet with rice as a composite dish in any approved source. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate using white steamed rice (jasmine-type) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked white rice (GI range 64–80, representative value 72; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The chicken, sambal, and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g / 100) / 100 = 72 × (17.39 × 5.0) / 100 = 72 × 86.95 / 100 = 62.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 242 mg/100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1210 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg, with additional sodium risk from belacan-based sambal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced or belacan-free sambal, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake from a single meal to below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice dominates the carbohydrate load (17.39 g/100 g; approximately 87 g per 500 g serving) with low dietary fibre (1.16 g/100 g), producing a glycemic load of approximately 63 per serving and rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, significantly reduce the rice portion, and increase the proportion of raw vegetables (cabbage, cucumber) to attenuate postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.64 g/100 g (18.2 g per 500 g serving) from deep-frying in palm or coconut oil, combined with cholesterol at 45.45 mg/100 g and trans fat at 0.09 g/100 g, presents a moderate atherogenic burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request grilled rather than deep-fried chicken to reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake, and avoid repeated consumption of this dish more than once per week.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam penyet with rice is an Indonesian-origin dish widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia, consisting of deep-fried chicken that is smashed flat with a pestle to tenderise the meat and create a crispy, fractured crust, served alongside steamed white rice, spicy sambal chilli paste, and accompaniments such as tempeh, tofu, sliced cucumber, and raw cabbage. The chicken is typically marinated in a spiced paste before deep-frying in palm or coconut oil, resulting in a golden, crunchy exterior with moist, flavourful meat. The sambal is made from ground chilli, tomato, shallots, garlic, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste), providing an intensely pungent, spicy, and umami-rich condiment. Nutritionally, the dish provides 193 kcal per 100 g, with 11.28 g protein, 8.7 g fat, and 17.39 g carbohydrate. The high-GI white rice component drives a significant glycaemic load per serving (approximately 63 for a 500 g plate), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content at 242 mg/100 g is borderline, with the sambal and belacan capable of substantially increasing total sodium intake. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. It is a popular lunch and dinner option at Malay food stalls, Indonesian restaurants, and hawker centres across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ayam penyet with rice?
Is ayam penyet suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of ayam penyet?
Can I eat ayam penyet if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of ayam penyet?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id F05100003) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Secondary micronutrients retained from input at lower confidence (0.70) and flagged accordingly in notes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate analogue, which is well-supported and fully documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite ayam penyet with rice dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Secondary micronutrients (calcium 14.55 mg, iron 0.73 mg, cholesterol 45.45 mg, saturated fat 3.64 g, trans fat 0.09 g, vitamin C 2.73 mg) were present in the input record at confidence 0.70 attributed to a non-approved secondary source; these are retained as indicative estimates pending verification against an approved primary source. The input portions array contained three entries with duplicate labels and two identical gram weights (550 g, 450 g, 550 g); a single representative serving of 500 g has been adopted as the canonical default, reflecting the midpoint of the observed range. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced beef meatballs as an ingredient; this is not a standard component of ayam penyet with rice and has not been carried forward into the classification. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white steamed rice per the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
